Psalm 137 confronts us with the raw reality of grief and exile. These are not the words of someone comfortably reflecting on their faith in a moment of doubt. These are the words of a people torn from everything they knew, sitting by foreign rivers with their harps hanging silent in the willows. The psalmist's captors mockingly demand songs of joy—demand that the exiles sing about Zion, about home, about the God they worship. But the question cuts to the heart of everything: "How can we sing a song of the LORD in a foreign land?" This is not a rhetorical flourish. This is the weight of real suffering asking whether worship itself becomes impossible when everything that anchored you to God has been stripped away. The silence of the harps speaks louder than any song could.

The psalmist's response reveals the depth of their covenant love. The oath in verses 5 and 6 is not poetic embellishment—it is a binding commitment of the entire self. If I forget you, Jerusalem, may my right hand lose its power. May my tongue freeze silent if I do not remember you above all my greatest joys. This is not nostalgia. This is the refusal to let exile redefine what matters most. Even separated from the physical presence of Zion, even surrounded by enemies who mock their faith, the psalmist anchors themselves to remembrance. In a foreign land where forgetting might bring comfort, remembrance becomes an act of defiance and worship.

The final verses shatter any sentiment we might attach to this psalm. The psalmist calls on God to remember the cruelty of Edom, who rejoiced at Jerusalem's fall. Then comes language so violent it disturbs modern readers—blessed is he who dashes Babylon's infants against the rocks. This is not a prayer the spiritually mature should sanitize or smooth over. This is a prayer born from unimaginable suffering, where a conquered people imagines retribution so complete it includes the next generation of their enemies. The Bible includes this prayer not because God endorses the sentiment but because He knows that human suffering runs this deep. He enters into the darkness of our rage and sorrow without flinching. We are invited to bring our whole selves—even our vengeful, broken selves—before Him.

What Psalm 137 refuses to allow is the comfort of half-faith. You cannot sing the LORD's song in a foreign land if you have forgotten what home means. You cannot worship if your heart has adapted to exile. The psalmist stakes everything on remembrance, on refusing to be remade by circumstance, and on holding God accountable for justice even when it seems deferred. This is the psalm for those who grieve deeply, who refuse to forget what has been lost, and who dare to ask God hard questions about suffering and vindication. It invites us not to neat answers but to the kind of honesty before God that makes genuine faith possible.
